# Microbial Inoculants in Agriculture

Shobhit Institute of Engineering & Technology, (popularly known as Shobhit Deemed University), is a NAAC Accredited, deemed to-be university established u/s 3 of UGC Act 1956. We are a research-based, industry oriented and highly ranked multidisciplinary University. Our academic programs are duly approved by UGC, and relevant statutory councils, i.e. AICTE, BCI and NCTE etc. Our strong Alumni Network, of more than 50000 graduates, extends throughout the world serving a wide spectrum of industries globally.

## Resource Person

![award1](images/de-01.jpg)

#### Dr. Ekta Narwal

Assistant professor  
Department of Agriculture and Agri-informatics  
School of Biological Engineering & Life Sciences  
Shobhit Institute of Engineering & Technology  
A NAAC Accredited Deemed to-be University) Meerut

Dr. Ekta Narwal is a University Gold Medalist (UG and PG) from CCS University, Meerut. CSIR-UGC NET (AIR-37), GATE, ARS-NET qualified. Recipient of prestigious DST-INSPIRE fellowship for doctoral research programme. Ph.D. (Microbiology), Indian Agricultural Research Institute, New Delhi, Recipient of Young Microbiologist award at ATDS-2020. Recipient of Award of honour and recognition for Outstanding Performance in the India Book of Records and Asia Book of Records. Publications: Four abstracts in various international conferences, five book chapters, one technical bulletin, four research papers and co-author in one book.

Interested Areas: Microbiology, Agricultural Microbiology, Plant-Microbe Interaction, Mycorrhiza, Bioremediation, Biodegradation, Microbial Genetics, Microbial Biotechnology

© 2021 Shobhit University.

| --- |
| **Progress** 07/15/24 to 11/14/24  **Outputs** Target Audience:In the scientific aims of Dr. Custer's proposal, he will engage growers on the eastern shore of Maryland and Deleware. This area is home to many economically disadvantaged growers who would benefit from longer lasting benefits of bioinculuants. Beyond the scientific aims of his proposal, Dr. Custer is working to develop a set of outreach activities to engage disadvantagedsecondary school students and introduce the importance of soil microbes for promoting stress tolerance. This effort aligns with the third aim of Custer's proposal. At his new institution, Custer has fostered a relationship with Mr. Jerry Kelly, the director of Parkside High School's A+ Garden Centre. This program is the largest high school garden enterprise in the United States and is funded through the Wicomico Country Career and Technical Education program. Parkside High School is located in Salisbury, MD, and serves a mixture ofinner-city and rural students.As part of their certification, students are encouraged to engage in scholarly research to enhance horticultural productivity in the plant system of their choice. Custer has worked with his new institution to integrate two high school interns into this research program. They will begin working on the project in the spring of 2025. Changes/Problems:The only challengeso far was the result of accepting a tenure-track job. Moving to my new institution, coupled with the timeline of project funding,prevented me from implementing the greenhouse component of this research in the first summer. Due to the timeline of my move and when the award was approved, I could not plant wheat during this growing season. To begin this portion of the proposed research, I have scheduled greenhouse space for the spring of 2025. This is a temporary challenge thatwill be rectified during thespring 2025. What opportunities for training and professional development has the project provided?This fellowship has given Dr. Custer first-hand experience with project management, financial planning, and the required reporting. He is currently working to implement the proposed research at his new institution and has also worked through the process of transferring a grant between institutions. His continued work on this project will culminate with peer-reviewed publications, and his work with Mr. Kelly will facilitate continued outreach capacity in his lab group. The relationship with Mr. Kelly will promote training opportunities for local high school students. How have the results been disseminated to communities of interest? Nothing Reported What do you plan to do during the next reporting period to accomplish the goals? Nothing Reported   **Impacts** What was accomplished under these goals? Obj. 1 & 2 - Improving AMF and bacterial inoculations for enhanced phosphorous uptake by plants Custer ran preliminary trials to identify the rates for biological inoculant application in phosphorous-depleted soils. Along with an undergraduate student, Dr. Custer carried out an additional trial using the Arabidopsis thaliana Fro22 knockout to gain insight into the ability of phosphorous-solubilizing bacterial inoculants to alleviate iron stress. Preliminary results suggest that a single application of commercially available phosphorous-solubilizing bacteria at low rates can promote iron uptake. In addition, Dr. Custer has worked to isolate and identify the bacterial and fungal inoculants described in his proposal using molecular methods. Dr. Custer has worked with the greenhouse manager (Dr. Nadine Burton-Stubbs) at UMES Extension to schedule his first wheat planting for the spring of 2025 and the plants will run through the summer of 2026 in accordance with the adjusted project timeline. Obj. 3 - Outreach activity to increase the involvement of URMs in agroecology In this aim, Dr. Custer proposed to develop an outreach activity aimed at high school students. The goal of this activity was to increase high school student awareness of soil microbial ecology, agroecology, and soil sciences. While originally planned to be implemented in Pennsylvania, since his move to UMES, Custer worked with students from a local high school (Parkside High School, Salisbury, MD). This high school offers a certificate in Horticulture through its Career and Technology Education (CTE) program and runs the largest student-led greenhouse in the US (A+ Community Gardens, Salisbury, MD). The program director, Mr. Jerry Kelly, has opened his doors and allowed Custer to interact with his students several times. To date, he has lectured to two sections of Mr. Kelly's class, reaching nearly 30 students. At this time, there are additional plans to offer research internships to his students at UMES. Students will help Dr. Custer carry out the research proposed as part of this grant.   **Publications** |
|

**Progress** 07/15/24 to 11/14/24  
  
**Outputs**  
Target Audience:In the scientific aims of Dr. Custer's proposal, he will engage growers on the eastern shore of Maryland and Deleware. This area is home to many economically disadvantaged growers who would benefit from longer lasting benefits of bioinculuants. Beyond the scientific aims of his proposal, Dr. Custer is working to develop a set of outreach activities to engage disadvantagedsecondary school students and introduce the importance of soil microbes for promoting stress tolerance. This effort aligns with the third aim of Custer's proposal. At his new institution, Custer has fostered a relationship with Mr. Jerry Kelly, the director of Parkside High School's A+ Garden Centre. This program is the largest high school garden enterprise in the United States and is funded through the Wicomico Country Career and Technical Education program. Parkside High School is located in Salisbury, MD, and serves a mixture ofinner-city and rural students.As part of their certification, students are encouraged to engage in scholarly research to enhance horticultural productivity in the plant system of their choice. Custer has worked with his new institution to integrate two high school interns into this research program. They will begin working on the project in the spring of 2025. Changes/Problems:The only challengeso far was the result of accepting a tenure-track job. Moving to my new institution, coupled with the timeline of project funding,prevented me from implementing the greenhouse component of this research in the first summer. Due to the timeline of my move and when the award was approved, I could not plant wheat during this growing season. To begin this portion of the proposed research, I have scheduled greenhouse space for the spring of 2025. This is a temporary challenge thatwill be rectified during thespring 2025. What opportunities for training and professional development has the project provided?This fellowship has given Dr. Custer first-hand experience with project management, financial planning, and the required reporting. He is currently working to implement the proposed research at his new institution and has also worked through the process of transferring a grant between institutions. His continued work on this project will culminate with peer-reviewed publications, and his work with Mr. Kelly will facilitate continued outreach capacity in his lab group. The relationship with Mr. Kelly will promote training opportunities for local high school students. How have the results been disseminated to communities of interest?
Nothing Reported
What do you plan to do during the next reporting period to accomplish the goals?
Nothing Reported
  
  
**Impacts**  
What was accomplished under these goals?
Obj. 1 & 2 - Improving AMF and bacterial inoculations for enhanced phosphorous uptake by plants Custer ran preliminary trials to identify the rates for biological inoculant application in phosphorous-depleted soils. Along with an undergraduate student, Dr. Custer carried out an additional trial using the Arabidopsis thaliana Fro22 knockout to gain insight into the ability of phosphorous-solubilizing bacterial inoculants to alleviate iron stress. Preliminary results suggest that a single application of commercially available phosphorous-solubilizing bacteria at low rates can promote iron uptake. In addition, Dr. Custer has worked to isolate and identify the bacterial and fungal inoculants described in his proposal using molecular methods. Dr. Custer has worked with the greenhouse manager (Dr. Nadine Burton-Stubbs) at UMES Extension to schedule his first wheat planting for the spring of 2025 and the plants will run through the summer of 2026 in accordance with the adjusted project timeline. Obj. 3 - Outreach activity to increase the involvement of URMs in agroecology In this aim, Dr. Custer proposed to develop an outreach activity aimed at high school students. The goal of this activity was to increase high school student awareness of soil microbial ecology, agroecology, and soil sciences. While originally planned to be implemented in Pennsylvania, since his move to UMES, Custer worked with students from a local high school (Parkside High School, Salisbury, MD). This high school offers a certificate in Horticulture through its Career and Technology Education (CTE) program and runs the largest student-led greenhouse in the US (A+ Community Gardens, Salisbury, MD). The program director, Mr. Jerry Kelly, has opened his doors and allowed Custer to interact with his students several times. To date, he has lectured to two sections of Mr. Kelly's class, reaching nearly 30 students. At this time, there are additional plans to offer research internships to his students at UMES. Students will help Dr. Custer carry out the research proposed as part of this grant.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Education programs are essential to inform farmers and agricultural professionals about the benefits and uses of microbial inoculants, enhancing their understanding and application in soil health.
Regulatory challenges can impede the development and approval of microbial products, delaying their accessibility to farmers and restricting innovations in soil health management.
Gaining market acceptance for microbial inoculants requires demonstrating their effectiveness, building trust among farmers, and addressing misconceptions about their use in sustainable agriculture.
